questions.text: Which cardiac arrhythmia is most commonly associated with early insufflation during laparoscopic surgery?
questions.explanation: Pneumoperitoneum can stimulate a vagal response leading to bradycardia, particularly early during insufflation.

questions.text: Which of the following is the most appropriate emergency position for a patient with suspected gas embolism?

answerOptions.text: C) Left lateral decubitus with head down
D) Right lateral decubitus with head down

questions.explanation: This position helps trap gas in the right atrium and reduces the risk of embolus entering the pulmonary circulation.

questions.text: Which of the following factors contribute to hypercarbia during laparoscopy?

answerOptions.text: A) CO₂ absorption from the peritoneum

questions.explanation: Hypercarbia arises from multiple mechanisms including CO₂ absorption, ventilation-perfusion mismatch, and limits of the buffer system.

questions.text: Which of the following is least likely to be a clinical sign of gas embolism?
questions.explanation: Gas embolism typically causes tachycardia and hypotension due to obstructed venous return, not bradycardia.

questions.text: Significant hemodynamic changes during laparoscopy may be caused by:
questions.explanation: Pressure, chemical changes, and positioning all contribute to altered preload, afterload, and cardiac output.

questions.text: Which of the following ventilatory changes is not typically caused by pneumoperitoneum?
questions.explanation: Pneumoperitoneum decreases FRC by pushing the diaphragm cephalad and reducing lung volumes.

questions.text: Pneumoperitoneum tends to have what effect on cardiac index?
questions.explanation: Cardiac output and cardiac index generally decrease due to increased afterload and reduced venous return.

questions.text: According to SAGES guidelines, which of the following does NOT increase a patient’s risk for venous thromboembolism during laparoscopy?
questions.explanation: Age > 40 is considered a risk factor. Other listed factors are included in VTE risk stratification.

questions.text: Which of the following is not a recommended strategy for preventing intraoperative hypothermia during laparoscopy?

answerOptions.text: C) A forced-air body surface warmer
D) Cold insufflation gas to preserve pneumoperitoneum longer

questions.explanation: Cold gas could contribute to hypothermia; warming fluids, air, and the room helps maintain normothermia.

questions.text: Which insufflation gas is associated with reduced cardiopulmonary effects but increased fire risk?
questions.explanation: Nitrous oxide causes less acidosis but does not suppress combustion and should be avoided in cases of suspected bowel perforation.

questions.text: Why are inert gases like helium and argon not commonly used for pneumoperitoneum?

answerOptions.text: B) They cause rapid bowel distension
C) They are poorly soluble and increase gas embolism risk
D) They are absorbed too quickly

questions.explanation: Low blood solubility means these gases persist longer and increase risk of extraperitoneal accumulation or embolism.

questions.text: A patient develops bradycardia immediately after insufflation. What is the first step?
questions.explanation: Bradycardia may be vagally mediated. Immediate decompression often resolves the issue.

questions.text: What intraoperative factor leads to decreased renal perfusion and oliguria during laparoscopy?

answerOptions.text: D) Use of warmed IV fluids

questions.explanation: Elevated pressure compresses renal vessels, decreasing GFR and promoting ADH-mediated oliguria.

questions.text: Which of the following is true about warmed insufflation gas?

answerOptions.text: B) It reduces clinically significant hypothermia in all cases
C) It may reduce postoperative pain but does not significantly affect core temperature
D) It increases the risk of bowel fire

questions.explanation: Warmed gas shows small differences in temperature and pain but not large clinical impact for short cases.

questions.text: Which of the following is a recommended infection control practice for laparoscopic pneumoperitoneum gas?

answerOptions.text: A) Releasing gas directly into the room air
C) Evacuating gas into a suction container

questions.explanation: Pneumoperitoneum gas can contain aerosolized particles. It should be evacuated through a closed suction system.

questions.text: Which patient is at highest risk for VTE during laparoscopic surgery?

answerOptions.text: A) 22-year-old on oral contraceptives for acne
B) 35-year-old with BMI of 27 undergoing appendectomy
C) 50-year-old with cancer on hormone replacement
D) 40-year-old with recent uncomplicated upper endoscopy

questions.explanation: Cancer, hormone therapy, and age >40 are all risk factors. This patient is high risk and should receive tailored VTE prophylaxis.

questions.text: Which physiologic effect is most commonly observed during the first 15–20 minutes of pneumoperitoneum?

answerOptions.text: C) Rapid rise in end-tidal CO₂ and drop in pH
D) Increased tidal volume and minute ventilation

questions.explanation: The body absorbs CO₂ quickly after insufflation begins, before reaching steady-state elimination.

questions.text: If a patient becomes hypotensive during laparoscopy, what is the first recommended action?
questions.explanation: In the event of hypotension, stop insufflation and allow gas to escape. Then assess ventilatory status, volume status, and rule out bleeding or embolism.

questions.text: Which of the following increases the risk of VTE during laparoscopy?

answerOptions.text: B) Ambulatory patient undergoing small umbilical hernia repair

questions.explanation: Pelvic surgery and duration >1 hour are both VTE risk factors. These contribute to a moderate to high-risk profile.

questions.text: Extraperitoneal gas extravasation may present in all the following forms except:
questions.explanation: Gas extravasation is a pressure-related complication and may be subcutaneous, thoracic, or even embolic. Urinary retention is unrelated.

questions.text: Which of the following equipment safety practices is recommended when using CO₂ for insufflation?

answerOptions.text: A) Allow cylinder to lie on its side
B) Use tubing with visible condensation
C) Keep cylinder upright and use filtered tubing
D) Connect tubing directly to wall oxygen

questions.explanation: Upright cylinders prevent liquid CO₂ escape. Filters protect the insufflator and patient from contamination.

questions.text: What cardiac rhythm changes are most commonly seen during laparoscopy?

answerOptions.text: B) Premature ventricular contractions and sinus tachycardia

questions.explanation: These are common and typically benign changes during pneumoperitoneum, though they may require attention in cardiac patients.

questions.text: Which patient would be considered low risk for VTE according to SAGES guidelines?

answerOptions.text: A) 68-year-old with colon cancer on hormone therapy
B) 52-year-old undergoing laparoscopic oophorectomy with CHF
D) 45-year-old on dialysis undergoing pelvic exenteration

questions.explanation: This patient lacks VTE risk factors. Short operative time and no comorbidities indicate low risk.
